query: react state vs props responsibilities interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this React prompt, so broader React question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: Sharing State Between Components
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical React explanation of single-source-of-truth ownership and lifted state, which is the official baseline for props-owned versus state-owned data.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative React explanation of ownership, lifted state, and single source of truth.
theirGaps: It is more tutorial-oriented than bug-oriented for stale UI, cursor jumps, loops, and memo drift inside a single component boundary.

realWorldUseCases: When the same value is “owned” by both (prop + local state trying to represent the same truth), you create two sources of truth.
If two different places can write the “same” value, you will eventually get drift, overwrites, loops, or UI jumps.
followUpConfusion: Decide who owns it (parent via props, or child via local state/DOM), then design the API around that ownership.
Keep a single source of truth. Test by changing props and verifying UI updates correctly.

realWorldUseCases: The docs define single-source ownership clearly, but our page is more explicit about the production failure modes created by mixed ownership.
actionableExamples: The official accordion example remains a strong baseline for lifting state, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up boundaries between controlled, uncontrolled, and derived-state patterns once the same value appears in both props and local state.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny code example showing a child copying a prop into local input state, then drifting after a parent reset.

competitors.winReason: The React docs are authoritative on ownership, but our page is stronger for interview delivery because it names the exact multi-writer bugs teams actually debug.

competitors.label: State Scope
competitors.selectionReason: This rule page is a strong tutorial competitor because it focuses on state ownership, lowest-common-ancestor placement, and avoiding unclear ownership boundaries.
theirStrengths: It is practical about keeping ownership obvious and lifting state only when multiple siblings truly need it.
theirGaps: It spends less time on the concrete symptoms that appear when a prop-derived local copy starts racing the parent.

realWorldUseCases: Common symptoms (stale UI, input jumps, infinite loops, memo bugs)
followUpConfusion: Props are inputs owned by the parent. State is local memory owned by the component.
Decide who owns it (parent via props, or child via local state/DOM)

realWorldUseCases: The style guide covers ownership well, but our page is more incident-driven about drift, overwrite races, and cursor-jump failures.
actionableExamples: Its lowest-common-ancestor examples are useful, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on mixed ownership inside one component, especially around derived state and controlled versus uncontrolled inputs.

actionableExamples: Keep one short broken-versus-fixed example around copying props into local state so the ownership rule stays concrete.

competitors.winReason: This tutorial is strong on ownership placement, but our page is stronger because it turns the abstract rule into the exact bugs caused by duplicate writers.

competitors.label: Unable to pass props from parent to child and save it in state of child component
competitors.selectionReason: This community thread is a direct practical reference for the anti-pattern of copying props into state and then struggling to keep them in sync.
theirStrengths: It states the anti-pattern plainly: copying props into state creates redundant state that is hard to keep in sync.
theirGaps: It is narrower than the broader interview question about local drafts, controlled inputs, loops, and memo-related drift.

followUpConfusion: Duplicating source of truth in props and state.
Keep a single source of truth.

realWorldUseCases: The thread names the sync problem, but our page is stronger because it covers the wider set of symptoms beyond prop-copying alone.
actionableExamples: It still gives a direct before/after recommendation, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on when local state is still valid, such as intentional drafts, and how to decide who owns the authoritative value.

actionableExamples: Add a minimal local-draft example later so the difference between intentional draft state and accidental duplication is even sharper.

competitors.winReason: The community thread states the anti-pattern directly, but our page is stronger because it explains the full single-source-of-truth failure surface.
